g.data-narration: Welcome to our lesson on the Ethereum Virtual Machine - the heart that powers all smart contracts on Ethereum.
The EVM is a virtual machine that executes smart contract bytecode across all Ethereum nodes, ensuring consistent state changes.
g.text: A stack-based virtual machine that executes bytecode compiled from
Solidity/Vyper. Exists on every node, enabling deterministic and sandboxed

g.data-narration: Three key properties make the EVM unique: deterministic results, sandboxed execution, and quasi-Turing completeness with gas limits.
The EVM uses a stack-based architecture with 256-bit words and maximum depth of 1024 items. Operations pop from and push to the stack.
EVM has three data areas: stack (volatile, cheap), memory (volatile byte-addressable), and storage (persistent, expensive).
Gas measures computational effort. Every opcode has a cost, preventing infinite loops and compensating validators for their resources.
g.text: Every EVM operation consumes gas (measured in units). Users pay in ETH for
the computational work done. This prevents infinite loops and DOS attacks.

g.data-narration: EVM understands opcodes - low-level instructions. Categories include arithmetic, memory/storage, control flow, and environmental operations.
The EVM execution context provides access to transaction and block data like msg.sender, msg.value, block.number, and block.timestamp.
g.text: Smart contracts can access these variables during execution:

g.data-narration: A transaction flows from user → network → EVM execution on every node → consensus on final state → world state update.
g.text: EVM: Decodes calldata → Loads bytecode → Processes opcodes → Updates memory/storage → Computes gas

g.data-narration: Every node runs EVM locally and reaches the same result. This deterministic execution ensures the entire network agrees on state changes.
g.text: Deterministic execution means all honest nodes
compute identical state root and gas consumption.

g.data-narration: EVM-compatible blockchains like Polygon, BSC, and Arbitrum use the same bytecode and opcodes, enabling easy cross-chain deployment.
g.text: Same bytecode runs on multiple networks. Same opcodes, similar execution model:

g.data-narration: Let's trace a simple Solidity function through EVM execution: push values, add them, return result.
g.text: Example: Simple add(uint256 a, uint256 b)

g.data-narration: Key points to remember: EVM is stack-based, deterministic, uses gas, has three data areas, and supports 140+ opcodes.

g.text: Stack-based VM with 256-bit words, max 1024 depth
Gas prevents infinite loops and DOS attacks
Three data areas: Stack (temp), Memory (session), Storage (persistent)
140+ opcodes: arithmetic, memory, storage, control flow, environmental
EVM-compatible chains use same bytecode and execution model

g.data-narration: For exams: Emphasize determinism, explain why gas exists, contrast storage vs memory, and remember EVM is a state transition engine.

g.text: Focus on determinism - why consensus works
Understand gas as security + fee mechanism
EVM is a state machine transitioning world state
